questions.question: What are the six pieces that turn one attention layer into a stackable block?
options: Six identical attention layers
Embedding + positional signal, self-attention (masked in decoders), a position-wise FFN (4x expansion), residual connections, layer normalization, and cross-attention (decoder only)
Conv, pool, norm, dropout, dense, softmax
Tokenizer, encoder, decoder, beam, cache, head
questions.explanation: Every transformer since 2017 — BERT, GPT, T5 — inherits this skeleton; 2026 refinements (RMSNorm, SwiGLU, pre-norm, RoPE) polish the pieces without changing the shape.

questions.question: Why do residual connections matter for depth?
options: They reduce parameter count
Without x + sublayer(x), gradients vanish past roughly 6 layers — the residual stream gives gradients a direct path through arbitrarily deep stacks
They speed up the forward pass
They prevent overfitting on small data
questions.explanation: Depth is what gives the model capacity, and depth breaks without this plumbing — "attention is the star, everything else is the scaffolding that lets you stack it deep."

questions.question: How do encoder and decoder blocks differ?
options: Decoders have fewer parameters

Encoders are bidirectional (no mask, two sublayers: self-attention + FFN); decoders add a causal mask and a THIRD sublayer — cross-attention, where queries come from the decoder and K/V from the encoder, the only place information crosses over
Encoders process text, decoders images
They are identical except in name
questions.explanation: Pure decoder-only models (GPT) drop cross-attention entirely: just masked self-attention + FFN — the simplification lessons 06-08 specialize from.

questions.question: Why did pre-norm replace post-norm?
options: Post-norm was patented
Post-norm (LN after the residual add) is hard to train deeply without careful warmup; pre-norm (LN before the sublayer) trains stably at depth and is the 2026 default — Llama, Qwen, GPT-3+, Mistral
Pre-norm uses fewer FLOPs
There is no difference in practice
questions.explanation: A small reordering with big training-dynamics consequences: normalizing the input to each sublayer keeps the residual stream's scale free to grow.

questions.question: What is the position-wise FFN's role in the block?
options: It mixes information across positions
A two-layer MLP applied independently at each position (W_2 · activation(W_1 · x), 4x expansion by default) — attention moves information BETWEEN positions; the FFN transforms it WITHIN each position
It normalizes the attention scores
It caches keys and values
questions.explanation: The division of labor in every block: attention routes, FFN computes. Modern variants swap the activation for SwiGLU but keep the role.
